text: Two weeks of detraining significantly reduces VO2max in endurance-trained athletes.
Two weeks of detraining significantly reduces maximal stroke volume (SVmax), contributing to decreased cardiopulmonary function.
Two weeks of detraining reduces isokinetic knee extensor strength but preserves knee flexor strength and muscle endurance.
Maximal heart rate increases significantly after two weeks of detraining in endurance athletes.
Body fat percentage remains stable after two weeks of detraining, while lean mass may increase due to enhanced anabolic hormonal milieu.

VO2max is significantly correlated with both maximal stroke volume (r = 0.6) and knee extensor strength (r = 0.6), suggesting detraining-related VO2max loss involves both hemodynamic and neuromuscular mechanisms.
Muscle endurance is maintained during short-term detraining periods even when muscle strength declines, suggesting endurance adaptations are more resilient than strength adaptations.
